title: Tuna pasta salad
subtitle: Penne with tuna, capers, and a sharp lemon dressing.
excerpt: Cold cooked penne tossed with tinned tuna, cherry tomatoes, capers, and red onion in a lemon and olive oil dressing. No mayonnaise. Ready in twenty minutes and better the next day.
sourceNotes: Tuna pasta salad became a British home staple as tinned tuna and dried pasta reached mainstream supermarkets in the 1970s and 1980s. The Italian combination of tuna, olive oil, and capers, present in antipasto traditions, was simplified into a cold pasta format that has since become one of the most widely made packed lunch dishes in Britain.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps in the fridge for two days. Add a drizzle of olive oil when serving leftovers as the pasta absorbs the dressing as it sits.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better made 30 minutes ahead so the pasta absorbs the dressing.

content.text: This is the lemon-dressed version, not the mayonnaise version. Lemon and olive oil produce a salad that stays fresher longer, coats the pasta more evenly, and does not go claggy in a lunchbox. Mayonnaise versions are also standard but are better served immediately.
Use tuna in olive oil rather than brine if possible: the oil carries more flavour into the dressing. Drain it but don't press it dry — a little tuna oil left in the tin is worth keeping.
A medium saucepan, a colander, and a large mixing bowl.

Cook {{pasta-dried}} of pasta in well-salted boiling water according to packet instructions until just al dente. Drain through a colander and rinse under cold running water until the pasta is completely cool. Shake off excess water.
Whisk {{olive-oil}} of olive oil with the lemon juice and zest, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper in a large bowl.

Add the cooled pasta, drained tuna broken into chunks, cherry tomatoes, cucumber, {{capers}} of capers, and red onion to the bowl. Add the olives if using. Toss everything gently. Taste for salt and lemon — cold pasta needs more seasoning than warm pasta. Serve immediately or refrigerate for up to two days.

items.cause: Not enough dressing, or pasta dried out after draining.
items.fix: Add a drizzle more olive oil and toss again. The pasta will loosen as it absorbs the oil.

items.cause: Cold pasta needs more seasoning than warm; the pasta also absorbs dressing as it sits.
items.fix: Add more salt and a squeeze of lemon. The ratio will shift as it sits — refresh before serving.

items.cause: Tuna in brine was used rather than oil, or it was pressed too dry.
items.fix: Add another tablespoon of olive oil. For next time, use tuna in olive oil and drain without pressing.

items.cause: Raw red onion can be very pungent.
items.fix: Soak the sliced onion in cold water for 10 minutes before adding. This removes the sharpness without losing the flavour.

content.text: Tuna pasta salad sits in the same practical register as egg mayonnaise sandwiches and prawn cocktail — popular, unfussy, eaten without ceremony. The Italian combination of tuna, oil, and capers that appears in tonnato sauces and antipasto platters travelled into British domestic cooking through the convenience of tinned tuna. Once dried pasta became a pantry standard in the 1980s, the cold pasta salad followed. The version here stays closer to its Italian origins than the mayonnaise variant that dominates supermarket chiller cabinets.
